title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Administration
content: Many of these medications are administered orally, once a day, in a co-formulated combination tablet. However, some medications are not yet in combination formulas, thus creating a large pill burden for patients who require a regimen of three or four drugs twice daily. The increased number of pills can decrease patient adherence, particularly for patients in resource-limited settings, patients with dysphagia, or patients who struggle with the financial burden of a HAART regimen. For patients who have dysphagia or trouble swallowing for any reason, there are liquid preparations or crushable tablets, though dosages may need to be adjusted. The Food and Drug Administration has recently been approving medications containing two or three-drug, a fixed-dose regimen in a single tablet. Combination medications with once-daily dosing are preferred as these regimens have been shown to increase patient compliance up to three times over more complex dosing regimens. [9] [29] [30]

title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Currently, the standard of care for a treatment-naïve patient with HIV-1 is a three-drug, highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) regimen that is started as soon as possible after a patient tests positive for HIV. A foundation of HAART is the administration of drugs that inhibit HIV viral replication at several stages in the lifecycle through different mechanisms to prevent viral resistance to any single agent. However, the selection of these drugs and the life-long treatment of a patient with HIV can be complex. Management of a HAART regimen is a multifaceted process that should be administered by, or in consultation with, a provider with specific training as defined by the HIV-Medicine Association of the Infectious Diseases Society of America. This approach is crucial to optimize patient care as studies have demonstrated provider experience positively correlates with improved patient outcomes.

title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Indications
content: The first therapy to work against HIV was the nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor zidovudine. The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved this in 1987. By 1996, research showed the advantages of combining medicines to treat HIV. Using HIV medicines for treatment is called antiretroviral therapy (ART). This form of therapy is recommended for all patients with HIV by the Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) and the World Health Organization (WHO). This daily treatment of multiple HIV medications is an HIV regimen. A typical initial HIV regimen includes 3 HIV medications from a minimum of two drug classes. Although this treatment is not curative, it can provide longer lives for patients and reduce HIV transmission.

title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Indications
content: Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) is a treatment regimen typically comprised of a combination of three or more antiretroviral drugs. HAART may also be called antiretroviral therapy (ART) or combination antiretroviral therapy (cART). A key cornerstone of HAART is the co-administration of different drugs that inhibit viral replication by several mechanisms so that the propagation of a virus with resistance to a single agent becomes inhibited by the action of the other two agents. Management of a HAART regimen is a multifaceted process that should be administered by, or in consultation with, a provider with specific training as defined by the Infectious Diseases Society of America. This approach is central to optimizing patient care, as studies have demonstrated provider experience positively correlates with improved patient outcomes. [1] [2] [3] [4]

title: Art Therapy -- Clinical Significance -- HIV/AIDs
content: Despite the advent of antiretroviral therapy, HIV/AIDS patients often seek alternative nonpharmacologic therapies to relieve debilitating symptoms associated with their chronic illness. Along with physical suffering, HIV/AIDs, patients can experience ample emotional suffering that accompanies the negative stigma associated with their disease. The AIDS Memorial Quilt served was one of the first objective forms of art therapy and emotional expression for those people living with HIV/AIDs. [19] Art therapy in HIV/AIDs patients has demonstrated a positive impact on emotional and physical well-being. One small, non-randomized group study found improvements in depression scores and health-related quality of life in 25 HIV/AIDs patients after participating in individual or group art therapy sessions. [5] Another randomized study found better coping mechanisms and perception of physical symptoms in those who participated in a one-hour art therapy session versus those who solely viewed a videotape on art therapy. [20]

title: Efavirenz -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Treatment with efavirenz can significantly improve the quality of life of patients infected with HIV, but its efficacy heavily relies on patient adherence. The neurologic adverse effects associated with efavirenz cause some patients to discontinue therapy. An interprofessional team of healthcare providers must become actively involved in caring for their HIV-positive patients. Key members of the team include pharmacists, physicians, advanced practice practitioners, nurses, and social workers. Obstacles to successful ART include a lack of social support and the financial burden of therapy. Healthcare providers must provide counsel regarding methods of coping with these adverse effects and promote medication adherence. Identifying appropriate opportunities for members of each discipline to provide care and encouragement is crucial to successful ART. [19]

title: Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome Antiretroviral Therapy(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Who and how to treat?
content: HIV antiretroviral therapy should always be done with combination regimens. Initial treatment usually consists of two NRTIs (usually abacavir/lamivudine or tenofovir/emtricitabine) in combination with a third antiviral drug from one of the following classes: integrase inhibitor, NNRTI, or protease inhibitor plus an enhancer (cobicistat or ritonavir). This is based on the proven efficacy of this regimen and its more favorable side effect profile. Alternative treatments may be considered in special patients.

title: HIV and AIDS -- Prognosis
content: Without therapy, HIV infections are invariably fatal. However, effective ART with sustained virologic suppression dramatically improves the clinical outcomes for patients with HIV. According to a meta-analysis from 2017, life expectancy in high-income countries is estimated to be 43.3 years if ART begins at 20, and 32.2 years if ART begins at 35. In low- to middle-income countries, life expectancy is estimated to be 28.3 years and 25.6 years if ART starts at 20 and 35, respectively. In all regions, regardless of income, life expectancy after starting ART has improved, reflecting improvements in therapy and management such as improved ART regimens, earlier initiation of ART, and better socioeconomic and adherence support. [92]

title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
content: A typical initial HIV regimen includes 3 HIV medications from a minimum of 2 drug classes. Although this treatment is not curative, it can provide longer lives for patients and reduce HIV transmission. This reduction of transmission has become a popular use of antiretroviral therapy for HIV-positive individuals with an HIV-negative partner. The successes of antiretroviral therapy have reduced HIV to a chronic condition in many parts of the world as progression to AIDS has become rare. Studies have found that the 3-drug regimen has led to a 60% to 80% decline in rates of AIDs, hospitalization, and death. By 2030, the CDC plans to implement a 90-90-90 plan (90% HIV diagnosed, 90% on therapy, and 90% suppressed). This activity describes the indications, contraindications, and use of HIV antiretroviral therapy and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in promoting their safety.

content: Healthcare professionals should work as an interprofessional team to ensure they have a comprehensive patient history before selecting a HAART combination. Nurses will help monitor therapy, note progress or lack thereof, and verify patient compliance with therapy, which is of paramount importance with HAART. As always, healthcare team coordination and patient education are critical components to maximize patient adherence, prevent further spread of disease, and provide continuity of care to the patients. content: The treatment of HIV requires the use of ART to suppress viral load and maintain CD4 counts. [15] Various drug combinations are used to treat HIV, and the treatment is for life. The specifics of ART are discussed in the HIV–HIV-antiretroviral therapy activity. Several infections or conditions have been described thus far in the reading; many of these conditions require medical or surgical management, very similar to that of the patient who was not infected with HIV. Therefore, those specifics will not be reviewed here. This piece will focus on the prophylaxis and treatment of opportunistic infections and disease states associated with long-term HIV infection or severe immunosuppression (AIDS). A mainstay of the management of most of these specific HIV or AIDS-related conditions and infections is ART to decrease viral load and increase CD4 cell count.

title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 35
content: ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE encompass a broad category of antiviral agents, within which direct acting antivirals represent a specific subclass. Direct acting antivirals include both the class of cyclic amine compounds and combination regimens used for HIV treatment. The combination category “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” is itself a direct acting antiviral and comprises specific fixed‑dose regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine. The nucleoside analogue abacavir, which is chemically identified as dideoxyadenosine, is a component of both HIV combination regimens and is therefore mapped to dideoxyadenosine. Rimantadine, a member of the cyclic amine direct acting antiviral class, exerts a physiologic effect described as alteration of cellular synthetic activity. Together, these relationships illustrate how systemic antiviral therapy is organized into mechanistic subclasses, how specific chemical entities like abacavir relate to their nucleoside analogues, and how agents such as rimantadine produce defined cellular effects, informing both pharmacologic classification and therapeutic application.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
content: Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antiviral agents that target viral protease enzymes essential for the replication of certain viruses, most notably HIV. Key members of this class include fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which is classified as a protease inhibitor. By inhibiting the protease step in the viral life cycle, these drugs prevent the maturation of infectious viral particles and are integral components of combination antiretroviral therapy used to achieve viral suppression in patients with HIV infection. Understanding that fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir all belong to the protease inhibitor group helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, anticipate class‑related effects, and manage drug‑drug interactions within comprehensive HIV care.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
content: Direct‑acting antivirals constitute a broad pharmacologic group that is organized by therapeutic indication, mechanism of action, and anatomical site of use. title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
content: Fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir are all members of the protease inhibitor class, which constitutes a group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes, thereby blocking the proteolytic processing of viral polyproteins that is essential for viral replication. This mechanism makes them key components of antiretroviral therapy, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where inhibition of the HIV protease enzyme reduces viral load and slows disease progression. Recognizing that fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir share this common pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their collective role in disrupting viral life cycles.
